'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）とは、[[大阪府]] [[堺市]]堺区百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]([[円墳]])である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされ、国の[[史跡]]である。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の周濠に接して立地しており、陪塚の１基と考えられている。墳丘は径３５ｍ余り、高さ４、５ｍの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。１９１２年（明治４５年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ３ｍ、幅５４ｃｍの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀２～３、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉７、碧玉製棗玉４、碧玉製管玉７０、ガラス製小玉１０００、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している。大仙古墳の陪塚としては、内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。１９５８年（昭和３３年）国指定の史跡となっている。
==参考文献 == 松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』 株式会社東京堂出版 １９８９年 ３７４項
[[category:古墳]] [[category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[category:大阪府の古墳]] [[category:堺市の歴史]] [[category:大阪府の歴史]]

'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）とは、[[大阪府]] [[堺市]]堺区百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]([[円墳]])である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされ、国の[[史跡]]である。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の周濠に接して立地しており、陪塚の１基と考えられている。墳丘は径３５ｍ余り、高さ４、５ｍの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。１９１２年（明治４５年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ３ｍ、幅５４ｃｍの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀２～３、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉７、碧玉製棗玉４、碧玉製管玉７０、ガラス製小玉１０００、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している。大仙古墳の陪塚としては、内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。１９５８年（昭和３３年）国指定の史跡となっている。
==参考文献 == 松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』 株式会社東京堂出版 １９８９年 ３７４項
[[category:古墳]] [[category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] [[category:大阪府の古墳]] [[category:堺市の歴史]] [[category:大阪府の歴史]] [[category:堺区]]

'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）とは、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]（[[円墳]]）である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされ、国指定[[史跡]]である。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の周濠に接して立地しており、陪塚の一基と考えられている。墳丘は径35m余り、高さ4、5mの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。1912年（明治45年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ3m、幅54cmの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀2～3、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉7、碧玉製棗玉4、碧玉製管玉70、ガラス製小玉1000個余り、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している。大仙古墳の陪塚としては、内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。1958年（昭和33年）国指定の史跡となっている。
==参考文献 == 松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』 株式会社東京堂出版 1989年 374項

'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]（[[円墳]]）である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされる。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の東側、周濠に接して立地しており、同古墳の陪塚の一基と考えられている。墳丘は径35m余り、高さ4、5mの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。内部主体は木棺である。1912年（明治45年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ3m、幅54cmの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀2～3、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉7、碧玉製棗玉4、碧玉製管玉70、ガラス製小玉1000個余り、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している（出土品は[[宮内庁書陵部]]保管）。大仙古墳の陪塚のうち、内部主体、副葬品などの内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。1958年（昭和33年）国の史跡に指定されている。
==参考文献 == 松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』 株式会社東京堂出版 1989年 374項

{{日本の古墳 |名称= 塚廻古墳 |画像= |所在地= 大阪府堺市堺区百舌鳥夕雲町 |位置= |形状= 円墳 |規模=径35ｍ |築造年代= 5世紀 |埋葬施設=木棺 |被葬者= |出土品=銅鏡 碧玉製勾玉 鉄剣 |史跡指定=国の[[史跡]] |特記事項= }}
'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]（[[円墳]]）である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされる。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の東側、周濠に接して立地しており、同古墳の陪塚の一基と考えられている。墳丘は径35m余り、高さ4、5mの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。内部主体は木棺である。1912年（明治45年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ3m、幅54cmの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀2～3、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉7、碧玉製棗玉4、碧玉製管玉70、ガラス製小玉1000個余り、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している（出土品は[[宮内庁書陵部]]保管）。大仙古墳の陪塚のうち、内部主体、副葬品などの内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。1958年（昭和33年）国の史跡に指定されている。 ==1912年の発掘の顛末== [[森浩一]]は上記の長さ3メートルの中央のくぼんだ木板とは[[割竹形木棺]]の底部である可能性が高いが、丸木船が使用されていた可能性もあり、断定できないとしている。この1912年の発掘は土地所有者の主催で行なわれたものであり、必ずしも学術的な調査ではなく、坪井正五郎は呼ばれて立ち会い意見を述べた程度で、発掘の報告文も発掘を取材した新聞記者が考古学雑誌に寄稿したものが残っているだけだという。またこの発掘はわずか2日間で終了するあわただしいものであり、終了直後に、指定もれの陪塚が発掘されているという情報を受けた宮内省より中止命令が電報で届いたという。さらに、出土遺物の帰属をめぐり、政府と発掘した地主側とでしばらく裁判が続けられたという<ref>森（1965）pp.47-51</ref>。
==参考文献 == *松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』株式会社東京堂出版 1989年 374項 *[[森浩一]]「指定もれの陪塚」『古墳の発掘』中公新書65 中央公論社 1965年 47頁-51頁

'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]（[[円墳]]）である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされる。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の東側、周濠に接して立地しており、同古墳の陪塚の一基と考えられている。墳丘は径35m余り、高さ4、5mの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。内部主体は木棺である。1912年（明治45年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ3m、幅54cmの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀2～3、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉7、碧玉製棗玉4、碧玉製管玉70、ガラス製小玉1000個余り、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している（出土品は[[宮内庁書陵部]]保管）。大仙古墳の陪塚のうち、内部主体、副葬品などの内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。1958年（昭和33年）国の史跡に指定されている。 ==木板の性格と1912年の発掘の顛末== [[森浩一]]は上記の長さ3メートルの中央のくぼんだ木板とは[[割竹形木棺]]の底部である可能性が高いが、丸木船が使用されていた可能性もあり、断定できないとしている。この1912年の発掘は土地所有者の主催で行なわれたものであり、必ずしも学術的な調査ではなく、坪井正五郎は呼ばれて立ち会い意見を述べた程度で、発掘の報告文も発掘を取材した新聞記者が「考古学雑誌」に寄稿したものがあるだけだという。またこの発掘はわずか2日間で終了するあわただしいものであり、終了直後に、指定もれの陪塚が発掘されているという情報を受けた宮内省より中止命令が電報で届いたという。坪井と同じ東京帝大の国史助教授の[[黒板勝美]]は、塚廻古墳の報告文が載せられた考古学雑誌の同号に今度の塚廻古墳の発掘を厳しく批判し、古墳の発掘を制限する法令を施行すべきとの論文を寄せているという。さらに、出土遺物の帰属をめぐり、政府と発掘した地主側とでしばらく裁判が続けられたという<ref>森（1965）pp.47-51</ref>。
==参考文献 == *松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』株式会社東京堂出版 1989年 374項 *[[森浩一]]「指定もれの陪塚」『古墳の発掘』中公新書65 中央公論社 1965年 47頁-51頁

'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]（[[円墳]]）である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされる。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の東側、周濠に接して立地しており、同古墳の陪塚の一基と考えられている。墳丘は径35m余り、高さ4、5mの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。内部主体は木棺である。1912年（明治45年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ3m、幅54cmの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀2～3、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉7、碧玉製棗玉4、碧玉製管玉70、ガラス製小玉1000個余り、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している（出土品は[[宮内庁書陵部]]保管）。大仙古墳の陪塚のうち、内部主体、副葬品などの内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。1958年（昭和33年）国の史跡に指定されている。 ==出土した木板の性格と1912年の発掘の顛末== [[森浩一]]は上記の長さ3メートルの中央のくぼんだ木板とは[[割竹形木棺]]の底部である可能性が高いが、丸木船が使用されていた可能性もあり、どちらか断定できないとしている。この1912年の発掘は土地所有者の主催で行なわれたものであり、必ずしも学術的な調査ではなく、坪井正五郎は呼ばれて立ち会い意見を述べた程度というのが真相で、発掘の報告文も発掘を取材した新聞記者が「考古学雑誌」に寄稿したものがあるだけだという。またこの発掘はわずか2日間で終了するあわただしいものであり、終了直後に、指定もれの陪塚が発掘されているという情報を受けた宮内省より中止命令が電報で届いたという。坪井と同じ東京帝大の国史助教授の[[黒板勝美]]は、塚廻古墳の報告文が載せられた考古学雑誌の同号に今度の塚廻古墳の発掘を厳しく批判し、古墳の発掘を制限する法令を施行すべきとの論文を寄せているという。さらに、出土遺物の帰属をめぐり、政府と発掘した地主側とでしばらく裁判が続けられたという<ref>森（1965）pp.47-51</ref>。
==参考文献 == *松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』株式会社東京堂出版 1989年 374項 *[[森浩一]]「指定もれの陪塚」『古墳の発掘』中公新書65 中央公論社 1965年 47頁-51頁

'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]（[[円墳]]）である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされる。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の東側、周濠に接して立地しており、同古墳の陪塚の一基と考えられている。墳丘は径35m余り、高さ4、5mの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。近年の調査により周濠があったことが確認されている。内部主体は木棺である。1912年（明治45年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ3m、幅54cmの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀2～3、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉7、碧玉製棗玉4、碧玉製管玉70、ガラス製小玉1000個余り、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している（出土品は[[宮内庁書陵部]]保管）。大仙古墳の陪塚のうち、内部主体、副葬品などの内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。1958年（昭和33年）国の史跡に指定されている。 ==出土した木板の性格と1912年の発掘の顛末== [[森浩一]]は上記の長さ3メートルの中央のくぼんだ木板とは[[割竹形木棺]]の底部である可能性が高いが、丸木船が木棺のように使用されていた可能性もあり、どちらか断定できないとしている。この1912年の発掘は土地所有者の主催で行なわれたものであり、必ずしも学術的な調査ではなく、坪井正五郎は呼ばれて立ち会い意見を述べた程度というのが真相で、発掘の報告文も発掘を取材した新聞記者が「考古学雑誌」に寄稿したものがあるだけだという。またこの発掘はわずか2日間で終了するあわただしいものであり、終了直後に、指定もれの陪塚が発掘されているという情報を受けた宮内省諸陵寮（現在の宮内庁書陵部）より中止命令が電報で届けられたという。坪井と同じ東京帝大の国史助教授の[[黒板勝美]]からは、塚廻古墳の報告文が載せられた考古学雑誌の同号に今度の塚廻古墳の発掘を厳しく批判し、古墳の発掘を制限する法令を施行すべきとの論文を寄せているという。さらに、出土遺物の帰属をめぐり、政府と発掘した地主側とでしばらく裁判が続けられたという<ref>森（1965）pp.47-51</ref>。
==参考文献 == *松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』株式会社東京堂出版 1989年 374項 *[[森浩一]]「指定もれの陪塚」『古墳の発掘』中公新書65 中央公論社 1965年 47頁-51頁

revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称= 塚廻古墳 |画像=[[File:Tsukamawari Kofun.JPG|250px]] |所属=[[百舌鳥古墳群]] |所在地= 大阪府堺市堺区百舌鳥夕雲町 |位置={{coord|34|33|46.0|N|135|29|26.4|E|region:JP-27}} |形状= 円墳 |規模=径35ｍ |築造年代= 5世紀 |埋葬施設=木棺 |被葬者= |出土品=銅鏡 碧玉製勾玉 鉄剣 |史跡指定=国の[[史跡]] |特記事項= }}
'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]（[[円墳]]）である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされる。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の東側、周濠に接して立地しており、同古墳の陪塚の一基と考えられている。墳丘は径35m余り、高さ4、5mの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。近年の調査により周濠があったことが確認されている。内部主体は木棺である。1912年（明治45年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ3m、幅54cmの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀2～3、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉7、碧玉製棗玉4、碧玉製管玉70、ガラス製小玉1000個余り、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している（出土品は[[宮内庁書陵部]]保管）。大仙古墳の陪塚のうち、内部主体、副葬品などの内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。1958年（昭和33年）国の史跡に指定されている。 ==出土した木板の性格と1912年の発掘の顛末== [[森浩一]]は上記の長さ3メートルの中央のくぼんだ木板とは[[割竹形木棺]]の底部である可能性が高いが、丸木船が木棺のように使用されていた可能性もあり、どちらか断定できないとしている。この1912年の発掘は土地所有者の主催で行なわれたものであり、必ずしも学術的な調査ではなく、坪井正五郎は呼ばれて立ち会い意見を述べた程度というのが真相で、発掘の報告文も発掘を取材した新聞記者が「考古学雑誌」に寄稿したものがあるだけだという。またこの発掘はわずか2日間で終了するあわただしいものであり、終了直後に、指定もれの陪塚が発掘されているという情報を受けた宮内省諸陵寮（現在の宮内庁書陵部）より中止命令が電報で届けられたという。坪井と同じ東京帝大の国史助教授の[[黒板勝美]]からは、塚廻古墳の報告文が載せられた考古学雑誌の同号に今度の塚廻古墳の発掘を厳しく批判し、古墳の発掘を制限する法令を施行すべきとの論文を寄せているという。さらに、出土遺物の帰属をめぐり、政府と発掘した地主側とでしばらく裁判が続けられたという<ref>森（1965）pp.47-51</ref>。
==参考文献 == *松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』株式会社東京堂出版 1989年 374項 *[[森浩一]]「指定もれの陪塚」『古墳の発掘』中公新書65 中央公論社 1965年 47頁-51頁
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}}
==外部リンク==
[http://bunka8.nii.ac.jp/SearchDetail.do?heritageId=139017 塚廻古墳]（文化遺産オンライン）

revision.text:
{{Otheruses||大阪府河南町にある古墳|ツカマリ古墳}} {{混同|塚廻り古墳群}} {{日本の古墳 |名称= 塚廻古墳 |画像=[[File:Tsukamawari Kofun.JPG|250px]] |所属=[[百舌鳥古墳群]] |所在地= 大阪府堺市堺区百舌鳥夕雲町 |位置={{coord|34|33|46.0|N|135|29|26.4|E|region:JP-27}} |形状= 円墳 |規模=径35ｍ |築造年代= 5世紀 |埋葬施設=木棺 |被葬者= |出土品=銅鏡 碧玉製勾玉 鉄剣 |史跡指定=国の[[史跡]] |特記事項= }}
'''塚廻古墳'''（つかまわりこふん）は、[[大阪府]][[堺市]][[堺区]]百舌鳥夕雲町に所在する[[古墳]]（[[円墳]]）である。[[百舌鳥古墳群]]に属しており、すぐ近くにある[[大仙陵古墳]]の[[陪塚]]とされる。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
== 概要== [[大仙陵古墳]]の東側、周濠に接して立地しており、同古墳の陪塚の一基と考えられている。墳丘は径35m余り、高さ4、5mの円墳で、墳丘裾に埴輪列が確認されている。近年の調査により周濠があったことが確認されている。内部主体は木棺である。1912年（明治45年）に[[坪井正五郎]]により[[発掘調査]]が実施されており、長さ3m、幅54cmの中央のくぼんだ木板の上から、変形四獣鏡、変形五獣鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀2～3、硬玉、碧玉、ガラス、滑石製などの勾玉7、碧玉製棗玉4、碧玉製管玉70、ガラス製小玉1000個余り、滑石製臼玉数百などが出土している（出土品は[[宮内庁書陵部]]保管）。大仙古墳の陪塚のうち、内部主体、副葬品などの内容の明らかな古墳として重要である。1958年（昭和33年）国の史跡に指定されている。 ==出土した木板の性格と1912年の発掘の顛末== [[森浩一]]は上記の長さ3メートルの中央のくぼんだ木板とは[[割竹形木棺]]の底部である可能性が高いが、丸木船が木棺のように使用されていた可能性もあり、どちらか断定できないとしている。この1912年の発掘は土地所有者の主催で行なわれたものであり、必ずしも学術的な調査ではなく、坪井正五郎は呼ばれて立ち会い意見を述べた程度というのが真相で、発掘の報告文も発掘を取材した新聞記者が「考古学雑誌」に寄稿したものがあるだけだという。またこの発掘はわずか2日間で終了するあわただしいものであり、終了直後に、指定もれの陪塚が発掘されているという情報を受けた宮内省諸陵寮（現在の宮内庁書陵部）より中止命令が電報で届けられたという。坪井と同じ東京帝大の国史助教授の[[黒板勝美]]からは、塚廻古墳の報告文が載せられた考古学雑誌の同号に今度の塚廻古墳の発掘を厳しく批判し、古墳の発掘を制限する法令を施行すべきとの論文を寄せているという。さらに、出土遺物の帰属をめぐり、政府と発掘した地主側とでしばらく裁判が続けられたという<ref>森（1965）pp.47-51</ref>。
==参考文献 == *松村隆文「塚廻古墳」『日本古墳大辞典』株式会社東京堂出版 1989年 374頁 *[[森浩一]]「指定もれの陪塚」『古墳の発掘』中公新書65 中央公論社 1965年 47頁-51頁
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}}
==外部リンク==
[http://bunka8.nii.ac.jp/SearchDetail.do?heritageId=139017 塚廻古墳]（文化遺産オンライン）
{{デフォルトソート:つかまわりこふん}} *[[category:円墳]] *[[category:大阪府にある国指定の史跡]] *[[Category:百舌鳥古墳群]] * [[category:堺区の歴史]] [[Category:大阪府の古墳]]

{{Japanese Kofun
|name = Tsukamawari Kofun
|image = [[File:Tsukamawari Kofun.JPG|250px]]
| part_of = [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]]
|location = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] Mozu Yuun Town
|coordinates = {{coord|34|33|46.0|N|135|29|26.4|E|region:JP-27}}
|type = Circular Kofun
|map_size = Diameter 35 m
|built = 5th century
|burial = Wooden coffin
|Interred persons = 
|excavations = Bronze mirror, jade magatama, iron sword
|Historic Site Historic Site = National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]
|notes = 
}}

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|qq=|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|qq=|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|qq=|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|qq=|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|qq=|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|qq=|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|qq=|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

== Footnotes ==

== External Links ==

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|qq=|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|qq=|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|qq=|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|qq=|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|qq=|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|qq=|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|qq=|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

|built = 5th century

|Interred persons = 
|excavations = Bronze mirror, jade magatama, iron sword
|Historic Site Historic Site = National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]
|notes =

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

{{Japanese Kofun
|name = Tsukamawari Kofun
|image = [[File:Tsukamawari Kofun.JPG|250px]]
| part_of = [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]]
|location = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]] [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]] [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] Mozu Yuun Town
|coordinates = {{coord|34|33|46.0|N|135|29|26.4|E|region:JP-27}}
|type = Circular Kofun
|map_size = Diameter 35 m
|built = 5th century
|burial = Wooden coffin
|Interred persons = 
|excavations = Bronze mirror, jade magatama, iron sword
|Historic Site Historic Site = National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]
|notes = 
}}

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

{{Japanese Kofun

|built = 5th century

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. {{Japanese Kofun

| part_of = [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]]

|built = 5th century

|excavations = Bronze mirror, jade magatama, iron sword
|Historic Site Historic Site = National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]
|notes = 
}}

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, [[:en:Sakai-ku, Sakai|Sakai Ward]] of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]]. It is part of the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Kofun Cluster]], and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]].

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently [[:en:Archives and Mausolea Department|Imperial Household Agency burial Office]]) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

== References ==
* Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374
* {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently {{ill|Archives and Mausolea Department|en|Archives and Mausolea Department|WD=Q4346533|lt=Imperial Household Agency burial Office}}) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=kofun}} ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai|en|Sakai-ku, Sakai|WD=Q1042008|lt=Sakai Ward}} of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai City}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is part of the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Kofun Cluster}}, and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan}}.

== Overview ==
The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the {{ill|Archives and Mausolea Department|en|Archives and Mausolea Department|WD=Q4346533|lt=Imperial Household Agency burial Office}}). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan}}.

== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings ==
{{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently {{ill|Archives and Mausolea Department|en|Archives and Mausolea Department|WD=Q4346533|lt=Imperial Household Agency burial Office}}) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.

{{ill|Tsukamawari Kofun|ja|塚廻古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamawari Kofun|qq=}} {{Other uses||Kofun in Kōnan, Osaka Prefecture|Tsukamari Kofun}} {{Do not confuse with|{{ill|Tsukamawari Kofun Group|ja|塚廻り古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukamawari Kofun Group|qq=}}}}
{{Japanese Kofun |name = Tsukamawari Kofun |image = [[File:Tsukamawari Kofun.JPG|250px]] | part_of = {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Kofun Cluster}} |location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}} {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai City}} {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai|en|Sakai-ku, Sakai|WD=Q1042008|lt=Sakai Ward}} Mozu Yuun Town |coordinates = {{coord|34|33|46.0|N|135|29|26.4|E|region:JP-27}} |type = Circular Kofun |map_size = Diameter 35 m |built = 5th century |burial = Wooden coffin |Interred persons = |excavations = Bronze mirror, jade magatama, iron sword |Historic Site Historic Site = National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan}} |notes = }}
'''Tsukamawari Kofun''' (塚廻古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=kofun}} ({{ill|Circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) located in Mozu Yuun Town, {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai|en|Sakai-ku, Sakai|WD=Q1042008|lt=Sakai Ward}} of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai City}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is part of the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Kofun Cluster}}, and is considered to be an {{ill|accompanying mound|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/accompanying mound|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658220}} of the nearby {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}. It has been Historic Site1 as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan}}.
== Overview == The mound is located on the eastern side of the {{ill|Daisen burial Mound|ja|大仙陵古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen burial Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Daisen Kofun|WD=Q5849431}}, adjacent to its surrounding moat, and is considered to be one of its accompanying mounds. The tumulus is a circular mound with a diameter of just over 35 m and a height of 4 to 5 m, with a row of haniwa figures observed along its base. Recent investigations have confirmed the existence of a surrounding moat. The internal structure consists of a wooden coffin. In 1912 (Meiji 45), an excavation survey was conducted by {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}}, during which, from a centrally sunken wooden board measuring 3 m in length and 54 cm in width, a detypeed four-beast mirror, a detypeed five-beast mirror, an iron sword, 2–3 iron daggers, nephrite, jade, glass, 7 talc-made magatama, 4 jade date beads, 70 jade tubular beads, over 1,000 small glass beads, and several hundred talc-made mortar beads were excavated (the burial goods are preserved by the {{ill|Archives and Mausolea Department|en|Archives and Mausolea Department|WD=Q4346533|lt=Imperial Household Agency burial Office}}). Among the accompanying mounds of the Daisen burial Mound, it is significant as a tumulus with clearly defined internal structure and associated grave goods. In 1958 (Showa 33), it was Historic Site1 as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan}}.
== Nature of the Excavated Wooden Board and the 1912 Excavation Proceedings == {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}} suggested that the centrally sunken wooden board, measuring 3 meters in length as mentioned above, is likely the bottom of a {{ill|Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bamboo-split Wooden Coffin|1=split-bamboo-shaped wooden coffin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398019}}, although he also noted the possibility that a log boat was used as a coffin, and he could not conclusively determine which was the case. The 1912 excavation was carried out at the initiative of the landowner and was not strictly a scholarly investigation; in truth, {{ill|Tsuboi Shogo|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shogo|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425705}} was merely summoned to attend and provide his opinion, and the excavation report was nothing more than an article contributed by a newspaper reporter covering the excavation for an "Archaeology Magazine". Furthermore, the excavation was hastily concluded in just two days, and immediately afterward, a telegram was sent from the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja|宮内省諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Ministry burial Office|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (currently {{ill|Archives and Mausolea Department|en|Archives and Mausolea Department|WD=Q4346533|lt=Imperial Household Agency burial Office}}) ordering its suspension, following intypeation that an unregistered accompanying mound had been excavated. Additionally, {{ill|Katsumi Kuroban|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroban|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}—a lecturer in national history at Tokyo Imperial University, like Tsuboi—harshly criticized this excavation in the same issue of the Archaeology Magazine that published the report on the Tsukamawari Kofun, and he submitted a paper arguing that legislation should be enacted to restrict kofun excavations. Moreover, a lawsuit continued for some time between the government and the landowner over the ownership of the excavated burial goods <ref>Mori (1965) pp.47-51</ref>.
== References == * Takafumi Matsumura, "Tsukamawari Kofun", Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofun, Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989, p. 374 * {{ill|Koichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koichi Mori|1=Kōichi Mori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539977}}, "Unregistered Accompanying Mound", Excavation of Kofun, Chuko Shinsho 65, Chuo Koronsha, 1965, pp. 47–51
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== External Links == [http://bunka8.nii.ac.jp/SearchDetail.do?heritageId=139017 Tsukamawari Kofun] (Historic Site Online)
